chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Green Revolution — TNPSC Complete Lecture Notes 1){event. preventDefault();history. back();}" title="Back" style="position:fixed;bottom:24px;left:24px;z-index:9998;width:42px;height:42px;border-radius:50%;background:rgba(13,15,20,0. 92);border:1px solid rgba(245,200,66,0. 35);color:#f5c842;font-size:20px;line-height:1;text-decoration:none;backdrop-filter:blur(8px);box-shadow:0 2px 14px rgba(0,0,0,0. 45);display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;transition:all .

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: 2s;" onmouseover="this. background='rgba(245,200,66,0. 12)';" onmouseout="this. background='rgba(13,15,20,0. 92)';"> Legend: ⚡ CRITICAL ⚠ TRAP 💡 TIP 📋 PYQ 💎 GEM

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: ⚡ CRITICAL — Root Cause In 1960 , India faced a severe food shortage. The country was importing wheat from other nations — a sign of extreme agricultural backwardness. Famines were widespread, and the production of food crops like rice and wheat was critically inadequate. Pre-Green Revolution India was characterised by traditional farming methods with no modern technology. The government realised that without a dramatic intervention, food insecurity would be permanent.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: ⚠ TRAP — Common Mistake Students often assume the Green Revolution was announced by that name from the beginning. The programme was first launched as a different scheme, and the name "Green Revolution" came LATER — in 1966 . 1959–1960 Severe famine and food shortage in India. Wheat imported from foreign countries. Crisis triggers need for agricultural revolution. 1960 Intensive Agricultural Development Programme (IADP) — "Vazhi Nadatthum Thittam" (வழிநடத்தும் திட்டம்) — launched in 7 pilot districts only.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: 1966 The process proved successful. William Gaud used the term "Green Revolution" in a speech/article. The name was adopted officially and the programme extended to all of India. 2006–2007 Second Green Revolution launched due to resurgence of food insecurity. Target: increase food grain output from 214 million tonnes to 400 million tonnes by 2020 . Agricultural growth rate target: 5% to 6% .

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: ⚡ CRITICAL — Must Know All Four Names All four names below are examinable. Know EXACTLY what role each person played — they are frequently confused. Swaminathan 🇮🇳 Father of Indian Green Revolution An agricultural scientist who conceptualised and implemented the Green Revolution in India. He adapted Norman Borlaug's global ideas to suit Indian conditions. Later served as a Rajya Sabha MP — nominated during President A. Abdul Kalam's tenure in 2002 .

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: During the second Green Revolution (2006–07), he was in Rajya Sabha, giving him additional influence. CRITICAL Norman Borlaug 🌍 Father of World Green Revolution An American scientist who first developed the ideology of the Green Revolution globally. His concepts were studied and adapted by Swaminathan for India. It was Borlaug's framework that Swaminathan modified and applied with changes suited to Indian agriculture. PYQ William Gaud 🗣️ Person who coined the term "Green Revolution" An American who, in a speech/article in 1967–68 , used the p

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: The term was then officially adopted. He did not implement the programme — he only named it. ⚠ TRAP William Gaud coined the term . He did NOT implement the Green Revolution. Do not confuse him with Swaminathan or Borlaug. Subramaniam 🏛️ Agriculture Minister of India (at the time of Green Revolution) Served as the Agriculture Minister when the Green Revolution was being implemented. He provided the governmental and political support needed to execute Swaminathan's vision.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Without his backing, the programme could not have been implemented at scale. 💡 TIP — Memory Trick for the Four Names B orlaug = B ig world idea → S waminathan = S uited it to India → G aud = G ave it the name → S ubramaniam = S tate support (Minister) 📋 PYQ — Frequently Asked "Father of Indian Green Revolution" = Dr. Swaminathan "Father of World Green Revolution" = Norman Borlaug "Who coined the term Green Revolution" = William Gaud

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Tamil Name: வழிநடத்தும் திட்டம் (Vazhi Nadatthum Thittam — "Guiding/Pilot Programme") Year: 1960 Scope: Only 7 districts (pilot/trial) HYV seeds distributed to farmers in these 7 districts Chemical fertilisers (nitrogen-based) provided Irrigation facilities expanded Modern equipment (tractors, pump sets) introduced Pesticides provided Rationale for limiting to 7 districts: If the experiment failed across all of India, losses would be catastrophic.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Limiting to 7 districts contained the risk. ⚠ TRAP — IADP ≠ Green Revolution The IADP (1960) was the programme . "Green Revolution" is the name given to the process/outcome in 1966. IADP was not renamed Green Revolution — the term Green Revolution was coined for the agricultural transformation process triggered by IADP.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Year: 2006–2007 Context: Food insecurity resurfaced. India risked dependence on foreign countries again for food crops. Target 1: Increase food grain production from 214 million tonnes → 400 million tonnes by 2020 Target 2: Raise agricultural growth rate from 5% → 6% M. Swaminathan was serving as Rajya Sabha MP during this period (nominated during Abdul Kalam's presidency), giving the movement expert legislative backing.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: ⚡ CRITICAL — Core Concept HYV (High Yielding Variety) Seeds are the backbone of the Green Revolution. Understanding what they are and their limitations is essential.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Two different varieties of the same crop (e. , rice) are selected — each with a distinct superior quality (one may have high disease resistance; another high yield capacity). These two are cross-bred to produce a single hybrid seed that carries both qualities. This hybrid seed is called an HYV seed .

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: High yield — significantly more output per area Quick maturation — grows faster than normal seeds Requires large amounts of water (4× more than normal seeds) Requires chemical/nitrogen fertilisers to thrive More susceptible to pest attacks than natural seeds Cannot be saved and reused — seeds from HYV harvest do NOT produce the same quality in the next season

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.section: Crops Where HYV Seeds Were Introduced
chunks.text: Rice (Arisi / நெல்) — Primary focus Wheat (Kodumai / கோதுமை) — Primary focus Maize / Corn (Makka Cholam / மக்காச் சோளம்) — Secondary focus ⚡ CRITICAL — What HYV Seeds were NOT used for HYV seeds were NOT developed for: Oil seeds (எண்ணெய் வித்துக்கள்), Pulses (பருப்பு வகைகள்), or other non-food crops. The entire focus was on food crops only — specifically rice and wheat. 💎 GEM — Insight from Lecture The concept of HYV seeds came from Norman Borlaug as "Nutritive Seeds.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.section: Crops Where HYV Seeds Were Introduced
chunks.text: " Swaminathan studied Borlaug's concept and adapted it to Indian conditions with modifications. That's why Borlaug is "Father of World" and Swaminathan is "Father of India" — the latter made it work HERE. ⚠ TRAP — The Seed Reuse Problem A farmer who grows a crop from HYV seeds cannot take seeds from that harvest and replant them to get the same yield. The cross-bred advantage disappears in the second generation. Farmers had to keep buying new HYV seeds every season — this created economic dependence and was a major limitation of the Green Revol

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Cross-bred hybrid seeds introduced for rice, wheat, and maize. Provided high and rapid yield.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Synthetic nitrogen fertilisers applied to accelerate crop growth. Nitrogen causes rapid growth and higher yields. However, prolonged use degraded soil quality.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Chemical pesticides used to protect HYV crops from pests (HYV seeds are more vulnerable to pest attacks than natural seeds).

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: HYV crops require 4× more water than traditional crops. Extensive canal irrigation systems and pump sets were deployed. This led to a significant fall in groundwater levels.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Tractors, threshers (கதிரடிப்பான்கள்), pump sets and other modern agricultural equipment introduced. This boosted both speed and scale of farming. Manufacturing of these machines also increased, generating secondary economic activity.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Cattle, goats, and poultry rearing increased to support the agricultural ecosystem — for food, strength (draught animals), and utility.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Aspect Before Green Revolution After Green Revolution Wheat & Rice Production Low; India importing wheat Multiple-fold increase; self-sufficient Seed Technology Traditional local seeds HYV seeds — higher & faster yield Farm Machinery Manual / bullock-driven Tractors, threshers, pump sets Fertiliser Use Organic / natural manure Synthetic nitrogen fertilisers Rural Employment Limited agricultural jobs Increased village-level employment Food Security Famines; foreign dependence Self-reliance; no wheat imports needed Tractor Manufacturing Minimal G

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: The production of rice and wheat increased manifold. Rural living standards improved due to higher farm income and employment.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: 💎 GEM — Lecturer's Insight The Green Revolution was not without negatives. While it solved the immediate food crisis, it created long-term environmental and socio-economic damage. A complete answer in interviews must acknowledge BOTH its achievements AND its failures.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Farmers grew only rice and wheat — a monoculture . Diverse crops like oil seeds, pulses, and other food varieties were neglected. This extreme dependence on single crops is considered a major ecological failure.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Continuous planting of the same crop (no crop rotation) exhausted soil nutrients. Repeated use of chemical fertilisers raised soil alkalinity (pH levels), destroying soil micronutrients and microbial life. The soil "forgot" how to support other crops. 💎 Live Gem — Lecturer's Analogy The lecturer compared soil degradation to a skilled person who hasn't practised a skill for 10 years — they lose the ability. "Touch விட்டுட்டா" (lost touch). Similarly, soil that only grew rice/wheat lost its capacity to support other crops.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: HYV crops needed far more water than traditional crops. Farmers dug deep borewells and used pump sets aggressively, causing a severe fall in groundwater levels. This eventually led to water scarcity .

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.section: 4. HYV Seeds Cannot Be Reused (விதை மலட்டுத்தன்மை)
chunks.text: Seeds saved from HYV harvests do NOT produce the same high yield in the next season. Farmers had to buy new seeds every season from the government/suppliers, creating economic dependence and added cost.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.section: 5. Health Impact from Chemicals (உணவின் மீதான தாக்கம்)
chunks.text: Crops grown with heavy use of pesticides and synthetic fertilisers were found to have negative impacts on human health. Before the Green Revolution, only organic/natural manure was used.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: HYV seeds, chemical fertilisers, and modern machinery were expensive. Wealthy farmers could afford them easily; poor farmers could not. This widened the economic gap between rich and poor farmers.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: HYV crops are more susceptible to pest attacks than natural seed varieties. This meant more pesticides were needed, creating a cycle of chemical use and associated environmental damage.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Farmers planted only rice or only wheat repeatedly on the same land without rotating with other crops. This depleted soil nutrients as the soil lacked the diverse inputs from varied crops.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Many farmers lacked proper guidance on how to correctly use chemical fertilisers and pesticides. Improper application led to additional environmental and yield problems.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Parameter Before GR (Pre-1960) After GR (Post-1966) Seed Type Traditional / natural seeds HYV (High Yielding Variety) hybrid seeds Fertiliser Natural / organic manure Synthetic nitrogen-based chemical fertilisers Pest Control Minimal / natural Heavy use of chemical pesticides Water Requirement Normal irrigation needs 4× more water required for HYV crops Groundwater Level Stable Significantly reduced due to over-extraction Crop Diversity Varied crops grown Monoculture — only rice & wheat Soil Quality Naturally rich and diverse Degraded; pH alter

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: "The period during which new technologies were used to increase agricultural production worldwide is called the Green Revolution. " In India specifically: the use of HYV seeds, chemical fertilisers, pesticides, irrigation, and modern machinery to dramatically increase the production of rice and wheat. HYV Seeds High Yielding Variety seeds — hybrid seeds created by cross-breeding two varieties with distinct superior qualities to combine both in one seed.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: IADP Intensive Agricultural Development Programme — the pilot scheme launched in 1960 in 7 districts that formed the foundation of the Green Revolution. Monoculture Growing the same crop repeatedly on the same land without rotation — a criticism of Green Revolution agriculture. Crop Rotation Alternating different types of crops on the same land across seasons to maintain soil nutrients — this was largely absent during the Green Revolution. Nitrogen Fertiliser Synthetic fertiliser rich in nitrogen that accelerates crop growth.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Overuse leads to increased soil alkalinity and degradation. Groundwater Depletion Reduction of underground water levels due to excessive extraction via borewells and pump sets to meet HYV irrigation needs. Biodiversity Loss Reduction in the variety of species cultivated; in the context of GR, the neglect of pulses, oil seeds, and other diverse food crops. Cross-Breeding The process of combining two different plant varieties to produce offspring with the desirable traits of both parents — the mechanism behind HYV seed creation.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: 💡 Analogy: Upgrading from an Old Feature Phone to a Smartphone Imagine India's agriculture in 1960 as someone using a very old, basic feature phone. It barely makes calls. Then the Green Revolution happened — like upgrading to a smartphone. Here's how every part maps: India's 1960 food crisis → Your old phone dying — can't even make calls anymore. You're borrowing someone else's phone (importing wheat from foreign countries). Traditional seeds → Old phone battery — works, but slowly, barely holds charge.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: HYV Seeds → New high-performance battery — charges fast, lasts long, handles heavy usage. But if you try to copy it into another phone, it doesn't work the same (seeds can't be reused). Chemical fertilisers (nitrogen) → Turbo-charger or performance booster app — makes everything faster. But overuse drains the device long-term (soil degradation). Pesticides → Antivirus software — protects from threats. But powerful antivirus also deletes good files (kills beneficial microorganisms in soil).

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Tractors & modern machinery → Upgrading from manually typing to using voice commands and AI — everything is faster and more efficient. IADP — 7 pilot districts → Beta testing the new phone OS on 7 selected users before rolling out to all 1. 4 billion people. 1966 — Official Green Revolution launch → Official public release of the new OS — now everyone gets the upgrade. Monoculture (only rice/wheat) → Only ever running one app (WhatsApp) — all phone resources dedicated to it.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Other apps (skills) atrophy. Groundwater depletion → Leaving your new smartphone on max brightness and 5G constantly — great performance short-term, but battery health deteriorates permanently. Second Green Revolution (2006–07) → Your upgraded phone started slowing down after years of heavy use — so you do a major OS update (v2. 0) to restore performance. Income inequality in GR → New smartphones are expensive — rich people get the best model; poor people stuck with budget versions or can't afford them at all.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Q → Who is called the Father of the Indian Green Revolution? ⚡ CRITICAL 📋 PYQ Dr. Swaminathan is called the Father of the Indian Green Revolution. He was an agricultural scientist who introduced and adapted the Green Revolution concept for India, building on Norman Borlaug's global framework. Additional facts: He also served as a Rajya Sabha MP , nominated during President A. Abdul Kalam's tenure in 2002 . 💎 Live Gem There are TWO fathers to remember: Norman Borlaug (World) and M.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Swaminathan (India). The distinction: Borlaug invented the ideology globally; Swaminathan adapted and executed it specifically for India. Q → Who coined the term "Green Revolution"? ⚡ CRITICAL ⚠ TRAP William Gaud (an American) coined the term "Green Revolution" in 1967–68 in an article/speech. He used this phrase to describe the agricultural transformation that had already taken place. The name was then officially adopted in 1966 . 💎 Live Gem — TRAP Warning William Gaud did NOT implement the Green Revolution.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: He only NAMED it. Don't confuse him with Swaminathan (implementer) or Borlaug (originator of the concept). This distinction is a classic exam trap. Q → What is the IADP? What was its purpose? 📋 PYQ IADP = Intensive Agricultural Development Programme (Tamil: வழிநடத்தும் திட்டம் — Guiding/Pilot Programme) Launched in 1960 in 7 pilot districts of India. It was the first step of what later became known as the Green Revolution. HYV seeds, chemical fertilisers, irrigation support, and modern machinery were provided exclusively to these 7 districts a

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: The rationale for limiting to 7 districts: if the experiment failed, losses would be contained; if successful, it could be scaled nationally. Q → What are HYV seeds? What are their limitations? ⚡ CRITICAL HYV (High Yielding Variety) seeds are hybrid seeds created by cross-breeding two varieties of the same crop, each with a distinct superior trait, to produce a single seed carrying both qualities. Benefits: High yield, rapid growth, increased food production.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Limitations: Require 4× more water than traditional seeds Seeds from HYV harvests cannot be reused — farmers must buy new seeds every season More susceptible to pest attacks Were developed only for rice, wheat, and maize — NOT for oil seeds or pulses Q → What were the main criticisms / limitations of the Green Revolution? ⚡ CRITICAL Loss of biodiversity — monoculture; only rice & wheat grown Soil degradation — no crop rotation, chemical overuse, increased soil pH Groundwater depletion — excessive irrigation lowered water tables HYV seed sterili

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: When was it launched and what were its targets? 📋 PYQ The Second Green Revolution was launched in 2006–2007 by the Indian government to address resurging food insecurity and dependence on foreign nations for food crops. Targets set: Increase food grain production from 214 million tonnes to 400 million tonnes by 2020 Raise the agricultural growth rate from 5% to 6% Dr. Swaminathan was serving as Rajya Sabha MP during this period, providing expert legislative support to the second wave.

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Q → Why was the Green Revolution needed in 1960? 💎 GEM In the late 1950s and 1960 , India faced a severe food shortage. Rice and wheat production was critically low. India was importing wheat from foreign countries — a sign of extreme vulnerability. Famines were a real and present danger. There was no modern technology in agriculture. Swaminathan, then an agricultural scientist, recommended a structured approach to the government. His solution — the IADP — was piloted in 7 districts, proved successful, and was scaled nationally as the Green Re

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: 💎 Live Gem — Interview Insight In an interview or Group-1 exam, connect the background story: "What if the Green Revolution had NOT happened? " Answer: India would have remained permanently dependent on foreign wheat imports, famines would have continued, and agricultural backwardness would have stunted India's entire economic growth path. The Green Revolution wasn't just about food — it was about national sovereignty and self-reliance. Q → Who was the Agriculture Minister who supported the Green Revolution's implementation?

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Subramaniam was the Agriculture Minister of India when the Green Revolution was being implemented. He provided government backing for Swaminathan's scientific vision, enabling the programme to be executed administratively. The successful implementation of the Green Revolution in India is attributed to BOTH: Dr. Swaminathan (scientific vision) and C. Subramaniam (administrative & political support).

chunks.title: Green Revolution
chunks.text: Item Detail Green Revolution Year 1960 (IADP pilot) → 1966 (official name & national rollout) Father of Indian GR Dr. Swaminathan Father of World GR Norman Borlaug (American scientist) Who coined "Green Revolution" William Gaud (American), 1967–68 Agriculture Minister (GR era) C. Subramaniam Pilot Scheme Name IADP — Intensive Agricultural Development Programme Tamil name of IADP வழிநடத்தும் திட்டம் Pilot Districts 7 districts Main Focus Crops Rice, Wheat (primary); Maize (secondary) Crops NOT focused on Oil seeds, pulses, other non-food crops K

chunks.title: Five Year Plans & Economic Reforms

chunks.text: ★ Special Section: 1991 LPG Reforms ★ The 1991 Economic Reforms — LPG (Liberalisation · Privatisation · Globalisation) July 1991 · PM: P. Narasimha Rao · FM: Dr. Manmohan Singh · Crisis: BOP — Forex = $1. 2B only PYQ TNPSC 2018, 2019, 2021, 2022, 2023 — VERY HIGH FREQUENCY REFORM Context: Why the Crisis? BOP Crisis 1991: Forex reserves fell to $1. 2 billion — barely 2 weeks of imports. India was forced to pledge 67 tonnes of gold (Bank of England + Union Bank of Switzerland) to secure IMF loans.

chunks.title: Five Year Plans & Economic Reforms
chunks.section: Lpg — Five Year Plans & Economic Reforms
chunks.text: Narasimha Rao, FM = Dr. Manmohan Singh. Rupee devalued by ~25%. Three Pillars of LPG L — LIBERALISATION Abolition of Licence Raj (industries freed) Industrial de-licensing — from ~800 to 18 MRTP Act restrictions reduced FDI limits raised (51% auto-approval in 34 sectors) Import licensing abolished (capital goods) Reduced government control over economy P — PRIVATISATION Disinvestment of Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) Private sector entry in reserved areas SEBI given statutory status (1992) FIPB (Foreign Investment Promotion Board) establishe

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Full Name Jawaharlal Nehru Born 14 November 1889 Died 27 May 1964 Cause of Death Heart Attack (Maraadaippu) Age at Death 74 years Birthplace Anand Bhavan, Allahabad, Uttar Pradesh Father Motilal Nehru Mother Swarup Rani (Swaruprani) Spouse Kamala Kaul (married 1916) Daughter Indira Priyadarshini (born 19 Nov 1917) Sisters Vijayalakshmi Pandit · Krishna Nehru Hutheesingh Ancestral Home Anand Bhavan, Allahabad Education Trinity College, Cambridge · Barrister (1912) Known For First & Longest-Serving PM of India Years in Jail ~9 Years Children's Da

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Jawaharlal Nehru was born on 14 November 1889 in Anand Bhavan , Allahabad , in present-day Uttar Pradesh . Anand Bhavan was a grand family residence — and more than just a home, it is an important historical landmark. The family consisted of five members: father Motilal Nehru , mother Swarup Rani (Swaruprani) , Jawaharlal himself (the eldest child), and his two sisters Vijayalakshmi Pandit and Krishna Nehru Hutheesingh . His father Motilal Nehru was a renowned lawyer and a major influence in the Indian National Congress.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Motilal Nehru was the founder of the Swaraj Party (Swarajya Katchi) and remained influential in Congress until 1929 . His affluent background meant that young Jawaharlal was sent abroad for studies at just 15 years of age . Nehru is remembered with several epithets: Raja of Roses (ரோஜாவின் ராஜா) — he always kept a rose in his lapel or pocket, a habit he reportedly drew inspiration from George Joseph , an advocate from Madurai , who was a close family friend of the Motilal Nehru family.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: He is also called the Architect of Modern India (நவீன இந்தியாவினுடைய சிற்பி) and the Jewel / Treasure of India . Gem Nehru's habit of wearing a rose in his pocket was inspired by George Joseph , an advocate from Madurai (Tamil Nadu), who was a close family friend and major influence on the Motilal Nehru family. Trap Most textbooks mention only one sister — Vijayalakshmi Pandit. But Nehru had TWO sisters: Vijayalakshmi Pandit AND Krishna Nehru Hutheesingh .

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Krishna married a Jain named Hutheesingh (a prominent freedom fighter in the Jain community), hence the surname Hutheesingh was added.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Institution / Degree Year Subject Notes Trinity College, Cambridge (Cambridge University) Sent at age 15 (~1905) Natural Sciences (இயற்கை அறிவியல்) One of the finest colleges in one of the world's best universities; sent to Britain by his father Motilal Cambridge University — Degree 1910 Natural Sciences Degree Graduated from Cambridge Barrister (Bar-at-Law) 1912 Law (England/France system) Barrister is the highest legal qualification in the British/French legal tradition; both father and son were lawyers Tip Nehru went to Britain (England) — N

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: He studied at Trinity College, Cambridge . Don't confuse with the USA. His father Motilal was also a lawyer, which is why the family could afford to send him abroad at just 15. While in Europe for his studies, Nehru was deeply influenced by the Italian nationalist leader Garibaldi (Garibaldi — National Leader of Italy). He read about Garibaldi's speeches, which were full of passionate nationalist fervour. He also encountered the spirit of Bismarck — notably, Bismarck is remembered as the German unifier, and Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel is called th

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: " Mussolini and Hitler were also European contemporaries Nehru studied. This European exposure planted in the young Nehru the dream of freeing India from British rule.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Nehru's active political life formally began in 1916 when he attended the Lucknow Conference (Lucknow Session) . At this historic session, the Muslim League and Congress came together, and moderates and extremists also gathered under one roof. It was here that Nehru first met Mahatma Gandhi — a meeting that shaped Indian history. That same year, 1916 , he married Kamala Kaul . In 1919 , following the Jallianwala Bagh massacre , Nehru established a newspaper — The Independent .

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: That same year he was elected Vice-President of the Kisan Sabha (Kisan = Farmer/Agriculturalist in Hindi/Sanskrit; also used as "Kisaan Jam" in Tamil). Nehru participated in the Non-Cooperation Movement ( 1920 ), gaining his first experience of imprisonment, and spent approximately 9 years in jail during his lifetime. By 1923 , he was elected General Secretary of the Indian National Congress . One year later in 1924 , Gandhi assumed Congress presidency at the Belgaum Session .

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: In 1925 , Sarojini Naidu became Congress president — remember this order: 1923 (Nehru — Secretary), 1924 (Gandhi — Belgaum), 1925 (Sarojini Naidu). In 1929 , at the historic Lahore Conference , Nehru and his father Motilal together spearheaded the Purna Swaraj (Complete Independence) resolution. Jawaharlal was elected Congress President and the famous Purna Swaraj declaration was made. By this time Nehru had effectively become the head of the party, with his father Motilal also prominently involved.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: In 1937 , Nehru presided over the Faizpur Congress Session — notable as the first Congress session held in a village . This is a frequently tested fact. Faizpur (also spelled Baispur/Paispur in the transcript) is significant precisely because it broke the urban tradition of Congress sessions. In 1940 , Gandhi launched the Individual Civil Disobedience movement . Acharya Vinoba Bhave was the first person selected by Gandhi to offer individual satyagraha; Nehru (along with Pattabhi) was the second to be arrested in this movement.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Pattabhi and Nehru, though holding different ideologies, were close friends who sometimes quarrelled — but were ultimately political allies. After independence on 15 August 1947 , Nehru became India's first Prime Minister , appointed and sworn in by Lord Mountbatten , who was then the Governor-General. Nehru served as Prime Minister until his death on 27 May 1964 — making him the longest-serving Prime Minister of India to date . He served through approximately three election periods (1952, 1957, and onwards).

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: During his tenure he established the IITs (including IIT Madras, IIT Delhi, IIT Bombay, IIT Calcutta), launched the Atomic Energy Programme , and positioned India as a modern, science-driven state. He also articulated the Non-Aligned Movement policy — refusing to align with either the American (USA) or Soviet (USSR) bloc during the Cold War. Critical Nehru is India's longest-serving Prime Minister to date. He served from 1947 to 1964 — approximately three full terms — until death.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: No other PM has served this long. PYQ Faizpur Session (1937) — First Congress session held in a VILLAGE. This has been asked multiple times in Group 1, Group 2, and Group 4 exams. Do not forget the name "Faizpur. " PYQ Lucknow Session (1916) — Nehru first met Gandhi here. Also the year he married Kamala Kaul. Previously asked question. PYQ Individual Civil Disobedience (1940) : 1st = Acharya Vinoba Bhave; 2nd arrested = Nehru (along with Pattabhi).

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Title Year Alternate Title Significance Glimpses of World History 1934 உலக வரலாற்றின் காட்சிகள் (Tamil) Letters written from jail to daughter Indira Priyadarshini; compiled as a book. A collection of letters tracing world history — written during his ~9 years in prison. An Autobiography 1936 Toward Freedom (சுதந்திரத்தை நோக்கி) His own autobiography written in 1936 — unusually early in life. Also known as "Toward Freedom. " Written while in jail.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Featured in old Tamil syllabus under "letters" topic. The Discovery of India 1946 இந்தியாவின் கண்டுபிடிப்பு (Tamil) Written to introduce India to the world; describes India's "Unity in Diversity. " Found in 6th standard school textbooks. Also found in Tamil translation. The Unity of India — — Extra/additional book to remember. Mentioned specifically by the lecturer as a lesser-known but important title to note. Critical Three books are absolutely must-know: (1) The Discovery of India (1946), (2) Glimpses of World History (1934), (3) An Autobiog

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Memorise years too — exams test them. Tip Mnemonic for books order: Glimpses (1934) → Autobiography (1936) → Discovery (1946). Each is roughly 10 years apart: 34 → 36 → 46. "GAD" — Glimpses, Autobiography, Discovery. Gem Nehru wrote his autobiography in 1936 — very early in life, when he was only 46-47 years old and had decades of life ahead. This is unusual. Note: most people write autobiographies near the end of their life; Nehru's was written in the middle of his political career.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Trap An Autobiography is ALSO called "Toward Freedom" (சுதந்திரத்தை நோக்கி). These are the SAME book with two names. Don't count them as two separate books.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Newspaper Year Founded Context The Independent 1919 Founded the same year as Jallianwala Bagh massacre; name foreshadowed the Purna Swaraj demand of 1929 by 10 years National Herald — Second newspaper associated with Nehru; both names to be remembered together Tip Only TWO newspapers: The Independent (1919) and National Herald . Note: "The Independent" in 1919 — the idea of independence came 10 years before the actual Purna Swaraj resolution (1929).

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Organisation Year Nehru's Role Notes Indian National Congress Member; active from ~1916 General Secretary (1923), President (1929, Lahore), PM representing Congress Devoted his entire life to Congress; Congress was founded in 1885 — Nehru born 4 years later in 1889 Kisan Sabha (Farmers' Union) 1919 Vice-President Kisan = Farmer (Hindi/Sanskrit). Elected Vice-President 1919 Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) Post-independence Architect / Co-founder Policy of not aligning with USA or USSR bloc during Cold War.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: India's diplomatic cornerstone. IITs (Indian Institutes of Technology) Post-1947 Visionary Founder IIT Madras, IIT Delhi, IIT Bombay, IIT Calcutta, IIT Kozhikode etc. — all seeded by Nehru

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: The Nehru family resided in Anand Bhavan , Allahabad . The five-member household comprised: Motilal Nehru (father, lawyer, Swaraj Party founder), Swarup Rani (mother), Jawaharlal Nehru (eldest, the future PM), Vijayalakshmi Pandit (sister), and Krishna Nehru Hutheesingh (sister). Vijayalakshmi Pandit — India's first woman diplomat; she had an excellent alliance with the USA , representing India in America. She was also the first woman to participate in foreign affairs at an international level.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Krishna Nehru Hutheesingh — married a Jain named Hutheesingh , a prominent freedom fighter in the Jain community. She met with Ben-Gurion , the first Prime Minister of Israel , representing India — making her one of the earliest Indian representatives to engage with Israel. (The lecturer notes this was likely Ben-Gurion, Israel's first PM. ) Indira Priyadarshini Gandhi — Nehru's daughter, born 19 November 1917 . Note: Indira Gandhi studied at Viswa Bharati University, Shantiniketan — the university founded by Tagore .

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Later she married Feroze Gandhi and became Prime Minister. She was known for a strong alliance with USSR (Russia) ; during the Bangladesh Liberation War , when Britain sent warships against India, the USSR dispatched its own fleet in India's defence. Nehru's Non-Aligned policy thus had practical benefits in both directions. Gem Indira Gandhi's full birth name was Indira Priyadarshini . She became "Indira Gandhi" only after marrying Feroze Gandhi.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Her birth year: 1917 , born on 19 November — exactly 5 days after her father's birthday (14 November). Gem The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) — while Nehru championed not aligning with either bloc, his own family members effectively maintained both alliances: Vijayalakshmi Pandit (USA side) and Indira Gandhi (USSR side), giving India a strategic advantage.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: 1885 Indian National Congress founded — Nehru would be born 4 years later 1889 Jawaharlal Nehru born on 14 November at Anand Bhavan, Allahabad, UP. Father: Motilal Nehru; Mother: Swarup Rani ~1904 Sent to Britain at age 15 to study at Trinity College, Cambridge (one of the world's finest universities) 1910 Receives degree in Natural Sciences from Cambridge University 1912 Obtains Barrister (Bar-at-Law) qualification — the highest legal degree in the British/French system.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Returns to India and begins law practice 1916 Attends Lucknow Session / Congress Conference — meets Mahatma Gandhi for the FIRST time. Muslim League and Congress unite at this session. Nehru marries Kamala Kaul same year 1917 Daughter Indira Priyadarshini born on 19 November 1919 Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurs. Nehru founds newspaper The Independent . Elected Vice-President of Kisan Sabha 1920 Participates in Non-Cooperation Movement ; gains first experience of imprisonment.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: First among prominent leaders to go to jail in this movement 1923 Elected General Secretary (Pothuch Chellaalar) of the Indian National Congress 1924 Belgaum Session — Gandhi takes over Congress presidency 1925 Sarojini Naidu becomes Congress president 1929 Lahore Session — Motilal Nehru and Jawaharlal jointly lead the Purna Swaraj (Complete Independence) resolution. Jawaharlal becomes Congress President 1934 Publishes Glimpses of World History — letters written to daughter Indira from jail, compiled as a book 1935 Government of India Act 1935

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Nehru sworn in as First Prime Minister of India by Lord Mountbatten (Governor-General). Delivers famous "Tryst with Destiny" speech 1948 Mahatma Gandhi assassinated — Nehru delivers tribute: "The light has gone out of our lives. " 1952 First General Elections — Nehru wins and continues as Prime Minister 1957 Second General Elections — Nehru re-elected Prime Minister 1964 Nehru dies on 27 May 1964 of a heart attack (Maraadaippu) at age 74 . Serving as PM until his death.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Contemporaries also dying: Muthuramalingam Thevar (1963–64), Kamarajar's tenure ends 1963 (Kamaraj Plan)

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: உலகம் முறங்கும் போது இந்தியா விழித்து கொண்டது. (At the stroke of midnight when the world sleeps, India awakes to life and freedom. ) — Nehru, on Indian Independence, 15 August 1947 (Tryst with Destiny speech) விதியோடு செய்து கொண்ட ஒப்பந்தம். (A Tryst with Destiny — spoken regarding both Independence and the India-Pakistan Partition) — Nehru, 1947 நம் வாழ்வில் ஒளி மறைந்து, எங்கும் இருள் சூழ்ந்து விட்டது. (The light has gone out of our lives and there is darkness everywhere.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: ) — Nehru, on the assassination of Mahatma Gandhi காலம் பொன் போன்றது. ) — Nehru (this phrase is attributed to him) [The Government of India Act 1935 is] ஒரு அடிமை பட்டயம். (A charter of slavery. ) — Nehru, on the Government of India Act, 1935 இந்தியா — வேற்றுமையில் ஒற்றுமை கொண்ட நாடு. (India — a land of Unity in Diversity. ) — Nehru, in The Discovery of India (1946)

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: People who influenced Nehru: Garibaldi (Italian Nationalist) Mahatma Gandhi (met 1916, Lucknow) George Joseph (Advocate, Madurai — rose habit) Motilal Nehru (father) Bismarck (European parallel) Mussolini (studied) Comparisons made by the lecturer: Bismarck of India = Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (NOT Nehru) Father of Pakistan = Muhammad Ali Jinnah Jinnah had cancer → died early → Pakistan unstable India stable because of Nehru's long tenure (3 terms) Key contemporaries mentioned: Acharya Vinoba Bhave Pattabhi (Sitaramayya) Sarojini Naidu Kamarajar

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: The connection: Nehru family ↔ Tagore's institution. Shantiniketan = Tagore's home AND university (Viswa Bharati).

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: PYQ India's First AND Longest-Serving Prime Minister — Jawaharlal Nehru. Served 1947–1964 (~17 years). Still the longest tenure. This appears repeatedly in Group 1, Group 2, Group 4 and UPSC. PYQ Birth date: 14 November 1889 . India celebrates Children's Day (Kuzhanthaigal Dhinam) on 14 November because it is Nehru's birthday. Nehru is called "Chacha Nehru" by children. PYQ Lucknow Session 1916 — Nehru met Gandhi here for the FIRST time. This is a direct previous year question.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: PYQ Faizpur Session 1937 — First Congress session held in a VILLAGE, presided over by Nehru. Asked once or twice in exams — memorise the village name "Faizpur. " PYQ General Secretary of INC: 1923. Nehru became General Secretary one year before Gandhi's Belgaum presidency (1924). Order: 1923 (Nehru Sec) → 1924 (Gandhi Belgaum) → 1925 (Sarojini Naidu). PYQ Purna Swaraj 1929, Lahore Session — Nehru and Motilal Nehru together. Nehru elected Congress President.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Important distinction: was it Motilal or Jawaharlal at Lahore? Answer: BOTH were involved; Jawaharlal was President. PYQ Individual Civil Disobedience 1940 : 1st = Acharya Vinoba Bhave; 2nd = Nehru (with Pattabhi). Frequently tested in sequence questions. PYQ Books : (1) The Discovery of India — 1946 — Unity in Diversity; (2) Glimpses of World History — 1934 — letters to Indira from jail; (3) An Autobiography / Toward Freedom — 1936. All three have been tested.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Don't miss The Unity of India as a 4th title. PYQ Vijayalakshmi Pandit — Nehru's sister; first Indian woman to participate in foreign affairs/diplomacy internationally; had strong ties with USA. PYQ Government of India Act 1935 — Nehru called it "a Charter of Slavery" (ஒரு அடிமை பட்டயம்). This quote is exam-relevant. PYQ Sworn in as PM by Lord Mountbatten (then Governor-General of India). Know that Mountbatten administered the oath of office. PYQ Nehru died on 27 May 1964 , of a heart attack, aged 74.

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: He was PM until his very last day. The same 1963–64 era saw deaths of Muthuramalingam Thevar, and Kamarajar's tenure as CM ending (1963). Trap Nehru studied in ENGLAND (Britain) , not America. He went to Trinity College, Cambridge . Some students confuse this with the USA. Trap Nehru had TWO sisters , not one. Textbooks show only Vijayalakshmi Pandit; the second sister Krishna Nehru Hutheesingh is often missed in answers. Trap When a question mentions "Nehru" in context of Lahore 1929 / Purna Swaraj — it may refer to either Motilal or Jawaharla

chunks.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
chunks.text: Read carefully. Motilal was also present but Jawaharlal was Congress President at Lahore 1929. Tip Congress founded 1885 . Nehru born in 1889 (= 4 years after Congress). Easy to remember: he was literally born into the Congress era, and devoted his entire life to it. Tip Nehru's death year 1964 clusters with other important events: Muthuramalingam Thevar's death (1963–64), Kamarajar's CM tenure end (1963, K-Plan), making this year a landmark in Tamil Nadu politics too.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Session Overview 10+ Leaders Covered 94 Years Periyar Lived 8 Newspapers Founded 1925 Self-Respect Movement Year Era Breakdown Pre-1857 — Social Seeds 1857–1920 — Reform Wave 1920–1947 — Periyar Era Post-1947 — Consolidation Key Themes: Anti-caste / Anti-Brahminism · Temple Entry · Self-Respect Marriages · Women's Rights · Dravidian Identity · Anti-Hindi Agitation · Rationalism · Social Justice CRITICAL Periyar is NOT a freedom fighter — he is classified as a Social Reformer of Tamil Nadu .

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: This distinction is frequently tested. Freedom fighters fought against British rule; Periyar fought against caste oppression and social inequality. TRAP The Madras Presidency produced more social reformers than any other province in India. While northern states produced more freedom fighters and religious reformers, Madras led in anti-caste social reform movements.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: B1 Jawaharlal Nehru ஜவஹர்லால் நேரு (பண்டிட் நேரு · சாச்சா நேரு) 1889–1964 National Leader First Prime Minister Born 14 November 1889 · Allahabad, UP Died 27 May 1964 · Heart Attack (Age 74) Title Pandit Nehru · Chacha Nehru · Jewel of India Father Motilal Nehru (INC Leader) Education Harrow School → Trinity College Cambridge → Inner Temple (Bar) Key Doctrine Panchasheela (1954) · Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) Key Book Discovery of India · Glimpses of World History · Autobiography Institutions Founded IITs · AIIMS · CSIR · Planning Commission PYQ F

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Children's Day = Nov 14 (Nehru's birthday). First PM: 1947–1964 . → View Full Notes

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: B2 Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji) சுபாஷ் சந்திர போஸ் (நேதாஜி) 1897–1945 Freedom Fighter INA Commander Born 23 January 1897 · Cuttack, Odisha Died 18 August 1945 (Disputed) · Plane crash, Taiwan Title Netaji (Respected Leader) Organizations Founded Forward Bloc (1939) · Azad Hind Fauj / INA (1943) Famous Quote "Give me blood, I will give you freedom" Key Contribution First to address Gandhi as "Father of the Nation" (1944, Singapore Radio) Key Book The Indian Struggle (1935) INA Trial Red Fort Trial (1945–46) — galvanized Indian independence movem

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Also said: "Jai Hind" — became India's national salute. INA = Indian National Army = Azad Hind Fauj. → View Full Notes

chunks.text: Ramasamy பெரியார் ஈ. இராமசாமி நாயக்கர் 1879–1973 Tamil Reformer Social Reformer Rationalist 🪪 Identity Full Name Erode Venkatappa Ramasamy (ஈரோட்டு வெங்கடப்பர் ராமசாமி) Born September 17, 1879 · Erode, Madras Presidency Died December 24, 1973 (Age 94) · Vellore, Tamil Nadu Father Venkatappa Naicker (வெங்கடப்ப நாயக்கர்) Mother Chinnathayammal (சின்னத்தாயம்மாள்) Mother Tongue Kannada (not Tamil — important exam point! ) Siblings (Brother) E. Krishnaswami (EVK) — ancestor of EVK Sampath & EVK Ilangovan (DMK) Sisters Ponnuthayee · Kannamma (Tamil

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: The second marriage to Maniammai (30 years younger) caused Arignar Anna and others to split and form DMK. CRITICAL September 17 = Periyar's birthday = Social Justice Day (சமூக நீதி நாள்) in Tamil Nadu. September 15 = Arignar Anna's birthday. These two dates are very close and frequently confused. 🏅 Titles & Nicknames Title (English) Title (Tamil) Given By / Context Periyar (The Great Man) பெரியார் First informally by Chidambaram Kudiyarasu (before official conferral); officially conferred November 13, 1938 by Neelambigai Ammaiyar (daughter of

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: after Vaikkam Satyagraha victory (1924) Rationalist Sun பகுத்தறிவு பகலவன் Honorific by rationalists Tamil Nadu's Rousseau தமிழ்நாட்டின் ரூசோ Comparative honorific The Caged Bird சிறைப்பறவை Due to multiple imprisonments 💎 GEM Who first called him "Periyar"? — NOT Neelambigai Ammaiyar. It was Chidambaram Kudiyarasu (சிதம்பரம் குடியரசு) who called him Periyar informally before the official 1938 conferral. TNPSC has tested this distinction! 🎓 Education & Early Life Had no formal higher education — self-educated through extensive reading and trave

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Combined with Bharati and other Tamil figures who visited Kashi and returned transformed, this period shaped his rationalism 1905–1908: Observed plague(புபோனிக் பிளேக்) epidemic relief work — single-handedly carried plague-affected corpses when even relatives refused 💎 GEM Periyar's Kashi journey parallels Bharati's Kashi journey — both Tamil luminaries visited Kashi and came back transformed. Bharati returned with the iconic headband (முண்டாசு) and twirled mustache.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Periyar returned with rationalist fire. Both stories show Kashi as a turning point for Tamil reformers. ⚡ Major Contributions CRITICAL Periyar's anti-God stance arose from a specific logical argument: "If seeing me pollutes the deity, then that deity does not exist" — his atheism was born from the injustice of untouchables being barred from temples. His God-rejection was essentially an anti-caste argument first.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Year Contribution / Event Significance 1902 First Samapanthi Bhojanam (inter-caste communal dining) 100 years before it became commonplace 1902 First inter-caste marriage ceremony presided Velalar girl + Naidu boy — across caste lines 1909 First widow remarriage arranged His sister Ponnuthayee's daughter (a child widow) 1914–1918 Organized Congress sessions in Erode Active in INC before split 1919 Municipal Chairman of Erode — Piped Water Supply First piped water to all homes in Erode; also improved sanitation 1919 Trichy Congress Conference Fi

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Travancore state removed temple approach road restrictions; earned title "Vaikkam Veerar" 1924 Cheramadevi (Sernthamadevai) Gurukul Incident Saw Brahmin students eat at tables; OBC students eat on the floor — this was the last straw for leaving Congress 1925 Founded Self-Respect Movement (சுயமரியாதை இயக்கம்) At the suggestion of S. Ramanathan; launched May 2, 1925 1925 Started Kudiyarasu (குடியரசு) newspaper Official organ of Self-Respect Movement; opened by Thiruppadiri Puliyur Gnaniayar Adigal & Thiru.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: 1927 Renounced "Naicker" caste surname Also stopped calling Gandhi "Mahatma" 1928 Started Revolt newspaper (English) To reach educated class; ahead of First Self-Respect Conference 1929 1928 Presided first Self-Respect Marriage — Aruppukkottai(Shuklanathham) No priest, no Sanskrit mantras, no Thali 1929–1931 Three Self-Respect Conferences (Chengalpattu, Erode, Virudhunagar) See dedicated section below 1932 Erode Plan (ஈரோடு திட்டம்) Suggested by E.

chunks.title: Study Material

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Imprisoned Periyar jailed twice by Travancore State Outcome VICTORY! Travancore state removed the restriction. All people could use temple approach roads. TRAP The Vaikkam Satyagraha was about road access near a temple (not entering the temple itself). The temple entry agitation for Travancore came later. Don't confuse these two separate events. chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Their relationship arc is a master story of Tamil political history: friendship → alliance → rivalry → mutual respect. 🔤 Tamil Block — தமிழ் குறிப்புகள் பிறந்த தேதி: செப்டம்பர் 17, 1879 · ஈரோடு — சமூக நீதி நாள் இறந்த தேதி: டிசம்பர் 24, 1973 · வயது 94 தாய்மொழி: கன்னடம் (தமிழ் அல்ல — தேர்வில் பொறி) சுயமரியாதை இயக்கம்: 1925ல் தொடங்கியது · அதிகாரப்பூர்வ இதழ்: குடியரசு (மே 2, 1925) பெரியார் பட்டம்: நவம்பர் 13, 1938 · நீலாம்பிகை அம்மையார் (மறைமலை அடிகளார் மகள்) வழங்கினார் வைக்கம் வீரர் பட்டம்: திருவிக்கா வழங்கினார் (1924) வகுப்புரிமை: திருச்சி, திரு

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) சக்ரவர்த்தி ராஜகோபாலாச்சாரியார் (ராஜாஜி) 1878–1972 National Leader Tamil Born December 10, 1878 · Thorapalli, Salem District Died December 25, 1972 · Age 94 (same age as Periyar! ) Titles Rajaji · Chakravarti Rajagopalachari · "Tamil Nadu's Gandhi" · "Dakshin Gandhi" (South India's Gandhi) Party INC → Swatantra Party (1959) ⚡ Key Contributions & PYQ Points 1920: Brought Periyar into Congress — crucial link between the two leaders 1937–1940: Chief Minister of Madras Presidency — introduced Hindi compulsory in 125 schools

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Periyar and Kamarajar both opposed this. Rajaji resigned in 1954 after massive opposition. 1948: Governor-General of India — the last Governor-General and first Indian to hold this post 1954–1963: Was not involved in Madras politics (was West Bengal Governor etc. ) Chakravarti Rajagopalachari vs Chakravarti Vijayaraghavacharya: Don't confuse! First supported Dravidanadu concept; later opposed it Founded Swatantra Party (1959) — free market liberal party PYQ TNPSC Group 1 (2024): "Who was called 'Dakshin Gandhi'?

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Rajagopalachari CRITICAL Rajaji was the Last Governor-General of India AND First Indian Governor-General (1948–1950). After him, the position was abolished and replaced by the President system. This is a very high-frequency TNPSC fact. 🔤 Tamil Block பிறப்பு: டிசம்பர் 10, 1878 · தூரப்பள்ளி, சேலம் இறப்பு: டிசம்பர் 25, 1972 · வயது 94 கட்சி: காங்கிரஸ் → சுதந்திரா கட்சி (1959) குலக்கல்வி திட்டம்: 1937–40 · பெரியாரும் காமராஜரும் எதிர்த்தனர் இந்தி திணிப்பு: 125 பள்ளிகளில் · பெரும் போராட்டம் இந்தியாவின் கடைசி கவர்னர் ஜெனரல்: 1948 · முதல் இந்தியர் இப்ப

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: 3 Arignar Anna (C. Annadurai) அறிஞர் அண்ணாதுரை · சி. அண்ணாதுரை 1909–1969 Tamil Leader Dravidian Politics Born September 15, 1909 · Kancheepuram Died February 3, 1969 · Chennai Titles Arignar (Learned Scholar) · Anna (Elder Brother) CM Tenure 1967–1969 (First non-Congress CM of Madras) ⚡ Key Points Suggested the name "Dravidar Kazhagam" to Periyar in 1942 (DK formed 1944) Was Periyar's disciple and close associate in Self-Respect Movement 1949: Split from DK → Founded DMK (Dravidar Munnetra Kazhagam) on September 17, 1949 — Periyar's birthday!

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Split reasons: (1) Periyar's second marriage to Maniammai (2) Political differences over elections 1967: Led DMK to power — ended Congress dominance in Tamil Nadu permanently As CM: Enacted Self-Respect Marriage Law (January 20, 1968) — fulfilling Periyar's dream Renamed Madras State → Tamil Nadu on Pongal (January 14, 1969) Famous for political oratory and dramas (like Parasakthi) CRITICAL Anna's birth date = September 15 . Periyar's birth date = September 17 .

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: DMK founded = September 17, 1949 (on Periyar's birthday). All three September dates are exam traps. Anna's political guru = Periyar despite their split. PYQ TNPSC Group 2 (Frequent): "When was Tamil Nadu renamed from Madras State? " → January 14, 1969 (Pongal day) , by CM Arignar Anna 🔤 Tamil Block பிறந்த தேதி: செப்டம்பர் 15, 1909 · காஞ்சிபுரம் திமுக தொடக்கம்: செப்டம்பர் 17, 1949 (பெரியார் பிறந்த நாளில்! ) முதல் திராவிட முதலமைச்சர்: 1967 சுயமரியாதை திருமண சட்டம்: ஜனவரி 20, 1968 தமிழ்நாடு பெயர் மாற்றம்: ஜனவரி 14, 1969 (பொங்கல் நாள்)

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: 4 Maraimalai Adigal மறைமலை அடிகளார் 1876–1950 Tamil Reformer Shaiva Scholar Born 1876 · Melaperumpallam, Nagapattinam Died 1950 Original Name V. Swaminatha Pillai Key Movement Tamil Purism / Neo-Saivism (தூய தமிழ் இயக்கம்) ⚡ Key Contributions Founder of the Pure Tamil Movement (தூய தமிழ் இயக்கம்) — removing Sanskrit loanwords from Tamil Despite being a devout Saivite, he was Periyar's ally in anti-Hindi agitation (1937–38) Periyar called Maraimalai Adigal and Ka.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Subramaniam Pillai his "two eyes" (இரு கண்கள்) during the anti-Hindi fight Daughter: Neelambigai Ammaiyar (நீலாம்பிகை அம்மையார்) — who conferred the "Periyar" title on November 13, 1938 Relationship with Periyar ended when Maraimalai returned to Saivism fully; their ideology differed on God 💎 GEM The paradox: Maraimalai Adigal was a deeply religious Saivite, yet he allied with atheist Periyar because both opposed the imposition of Sanskrit/Hindi and Brahmin dominance.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Their alliance shows that Tamil cultural identity transcended religious differences in Tamil political history. 🔤 Tamil Block தூய தமிழ் இயக்கம்: சமஸ்கிருத சொற்களை தமிழிலிருந்து நீக்கும் இயக்கம் மகள்: நீலாம்பிகை அம்மையார் — பெரியார் பட்டம் வழங்கியவர் பெரியாரின் இரு கண்கள்: மறைமலை அடிகளும் காசு சுப்பிரமணியம் பிள்ளையும்

chunks.text: Singaravelar இ. சிங்காரவேலர் 1860–1946 Communist Pioneer Tamil Labour Leader Born February 18, 1860 · Chennai Died February 11, 1946 Title Father of Communist Movement in Tamil Nadu Suggested the Erode Plan (ஈரோடு திட்டம்) — Periyar adopted it in 1932 First Communist / Socialist leader of Tamil Nadu Founded labor unions for textile workers and other laborers in Madras Organized first May Day celebration in India (1923, in Madras) Also worked for caste abolition alongside Periyar PYQ TNPSC (High Frequency): "Who organized the first May Day celeb

chunks.text: 8 Mahatma Gandhi (M. Gandhi) மோகன்தாஸ் கரம்சந்த் காந்தி (மகாத்மா காந்தி) 1869–1948 National Leader Social Reformer Born October 2, 1869 · Porbandar, Gujarat Died January 30, 1948 (assassinated by Nathuram Godse) Title Mahatma · Bapu · Father of the Nation ⚡ Key Connections to Tamil Nadu & Periyar Periyar was initially a Gandhi follower (1920–1927) — wore khadi, participated in Non-Cooperation 1927: Periyar permanently broke with Gandhi — "I will no longer call Gandhi 'Mahatma'" Gandhi called untouchables "Harijans" (children of God/Hari) — Peri

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: However, many SC leaders including Ambedkar and Periyar rejected this term as demeaning/patronizing. Don't assume "Harijan" was universally accepted.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: 9 Jyotirao Phule & Savitribai Phule ஜோதிராவ் ஃபுலே & சாவித்திராவ் ஃபுலே 1827–1890 Social Reformer Maharashtra Pioneer Jyotirao Born April 11, 1827 · Pune, Maharashtra Jyotirao Died November 28, 1890 Savitribai 1831–1897; India's first woman teacher Key Title Mahatma Jyotirao Phule · Father of Indian Social Revolution In the lecture: mentioned as pioneers of non-Brahmin movement in Maharashtra — they started it; Madras took it to a larger scale Jyotirao and Savitribai fought for women's education, anti-caste, widow remarriage in Maharashtra Peri

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: " → Savitribai Phule 💎 GEM The non-Brahmin anti-caste movement origin: Phules started it in Maharashtra → but it remained limited. The Madras Presidency took it to a nationwide scale with the Justice Party (1916) and Periyar's Self-Respect Movement (1925). Madras = epicenter of organized non-Brahmin politics.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Ambedkar டாக்டர் பீம்ராவ் ராம்ஜி அம்பேத்கர் 1891–1956 Social Reformer Constitution Architect Born April 14, 1891 · Mhow, Central Provinces Died December 6, 1956 · Delhi Title Babasaheb · Father of Indian Constitution 1936: Wrote "Annihilation of Caste" — Periyar immediately translated it to Tamil the same year Periyar and Ambedkar shared ideology on caste abolition but sometimes differed on methods 1956: Ambedkar converted to Buddhism; Periyar traveled to Burma (Myanmar) for Buddhist conference the same year Periyar said the Indian Constitution

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Both men shared anti-caste ideology and cooperated. The 1956 Burma conference shows their collaboration.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Based on pattern analysis of 2010–2024 papers. These 5 questions have the highest probability of appearing in 2026 Group 1. Who first used the term "Periyar" for E. Ramasamy before the official conferral in 1938? Chidambaram Kudiyarasu (சிதம்பரம் குடியரசு) ⚡ Why likely: This fact appears in Ka. Venkatesan's reference book but NOT in standard textbooks. Group 1 frequently tests such extra-textbook facts. Previously tested once; cycle suggests retesting.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: In which city did Periyar cut 500 trees for Gandhi's non-cooperation movement, and what type of trees were they? Erode — 500 COCONUT trees (thenai maram), not palmyra (panai maram) ⚡ Why likely: Classic TRAP question. Many candidates answer "palmyra" — the correct answer is coconut. Examiners test precise botanical knowledge. Toddy is extracted from both, but Periyar's grove had COCONUT trees. Match the Self-Respect Movement conferences: Chengalpattu / Erode / Virudhunagar → 1929 / 1930 / 1931 with their respective presidents Chengalpattu 1929

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: Shanmugam Chettyar ⚡ Why likely: Conference–city–year–president matching is a classic Group 1 format. Previously asked in Group 2; Group 1 will add the president names for difficulty. Periyar's book "Karppa Aatchi" deals with which subject? In which year was it published? Family planning / Birth control (குடும்ப கட்டுப்பாடு) — 1930 ⚡ Why likely: Asked in Group 2/4 multiple times. Now being elevated to Group 1 with additional context questions about its content or impact.

chunks.title: Study Material
chunks.text: The Dravidar Kazhagam was founded at which city? Who suggested the name "Dravidar Kazhagam" and in which year? Founded: Salem, 1944 . Name suggested by: Arignar Anna in 1942 (2 years before DK was actually formed) ⚡ Why likely: The 2-year gap between suggestion (1942 by Anna) and actual formation (1944 by Periyar) is a precision fact that Group 1 loves. Combined question format likely in 2026.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: 🪪 Identity — Core Bio Facts Full Name Subash Chandra Bose Title / Honour Netaji (given by Mahatma Gandhi) — means "Leader" (தலைவர்) Born 23 January 1897 · Cuttack, Odisha Died 1945 — Plane crash, Taiwan (Formosa) — DISPUTED Father Janakinath Bose Mother Prabhavati Devi Spouse Emilie Schenkl (Austrian) Daughter Anita Bose (born ~1942) Spiritual Guru Swami Vivekananda Political Guru C. Das (Chittaranjan Das) — recommended by Gandhi Education Cambridge University; ICS 4th Rank (1920) ICS Rank 4th Rank in India (1920) — resigned April 1921 Birthda

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: " 💎 GEM Bose FIRST called Mahatma Gandhi the 'Father of the Nation' (Desh ka Pita / தேச தந்தை). The title we use every day for Gandhi was coined by Bose — despite their ideological differences. CRITICAL INA was NOT founded by Subash Chandra Bose. It was founded by Mohan Singh and Rash Behari Bose (Ghosh). Netaji TOOK OVER command in 1943 in Singapore. This distinction is specifically tested in exams.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: 🏅 Titles & Nicknames Title Tamil Given By / Context Netaji (Leader) நேதாஜி Given by Mahatma Gandhi himself; means "respected leader" Parakram Divas பராக்கிரம் திவஸ் 23 January — national celebration of his birthday Father of the Nation (coined for Gandhi) தேச தந்தை (காந்திக்கு கொடுத்தார்) Bose FIRST called Gandhi by this title — historical fact Supreme Commander உச்ச தளபதி Commander of the Indian National Army (Azad Hind Fauj) Desh Nayak தேச நாயகன் Popular honorific used by followers

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: 🎓 Education & Early Life Born 23 January 1897, Cuttack, Odisha. Father: Janakinath Bose; Mother: Prabhavati Devi From young age, deeply inspired by Swami Vivekananda — his spiritual guru During school years, developed friction with British authorities Father Janakinath Bose sent him to London to avoid constant confrontation with British 1919: Left for London, England — went to Cambridge University Cambridge University: studied for ICS exam — only 8 months of preparation 1920: Wrote ICS (Indian Civil Service) exam — equivalent to today's IAS; e

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: He then resigned in April 1921, joining the freedom struggle instead. TIP ICS is the IAS of today. The lecturer's comparison: "It takes us 8 months to prepare for Group 4, but Bose cleared ICS (IAS equivalent) in 8 months. " Only London-based exams. Only wealthy families could attempt.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: ⚡ Major Contributions CRITICAL Bose resigned from one of India's most prestigious services (ICS/4th rank) to join the freedom struggle. After meeting Gandhi, he followed C. Das as political guru. He founded Forward Bloc (1939) with Muthuramalingam Thevar of Madurai, Tamil Nadu. Year Contribution / Event Significance 1920 ICS 4th Rank — Cambridge University After 8 months preparation; equivalent to today's IAS Apr 1921 Resigned from ICS posting Could not serve British and India simultaneously; met Gandhi who sent him to C.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: ⚡ INA & Azad Hind — Deep Dive CRITICAL INA (Indian National Army) = Azad Hind Fauj (ஆசாத் ஹிந்த் ஃபௌஜ்): 'Azad' = Free/Liberated, 'Hind' = India, 'Fauj' = Army. Founded by Mohan Singh + Rash Behari Bose. Netaji took over in 1943 in Singapore. Aspect Detail Original Founders Mohan Singh and Rash Behari Bose (Ghosh) — in Singapore/Burma Netaji's role Took over as Supreme Commander in 1943 in Singapore Full name Indian National Army (INA) / Azad Hind Fauj (Free India Army) Women's Regiment Founded by Netaji; Commander: Lakshmi Sahgal (doctor from

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: " (March to Delhi! ) — broadcast from Singapore radio Provisional Govt Azad Hind Provisional Government declared in Singapore (1943) — "Free India Government" Slogan "Jai Hind" — popularised by Netaji (original: Chembakaramam Pillai, Vienna, 1933; "Hind" suggested by Abdul Hassan, Hyderabad) Territory Operated from Burma/Myanmar and Singapore; moved through Axis-held territories

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: ⭐ Origin of "Jai Hind" Step 1 — 1933: Tamil man Chembakaramam Pillai (சேம்பகராமன் பிள்ளை) said "Jai Hindustan Ke! " at a conference in Vienna, Austria. He had close friendships with Hitler and Egypt's Nasser. (Previously in syllabus, now removed but still asked in exams. ) Step 2: Netaji, inspired by Chembakaramam Pillai, adopted "Jai Hindustan" as INA battle cry. Step 3: Abdul Hassan (Muslim, from Hyderabad, studying engineering in Germany) objected: "Hindustan sounds Hindu — Muslim soldiers won't join.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: " He suggested "Hind" (just India, no religious connotation). Netaji agreed → "Jai Hind" became the official slogan. CRITICAL Two exam questions, two different answers: (1) "Who FIRST gave Jai Hind? " → Chembakaramam Pillai (Tamil, Vienna, 1933). (2) "Who SPREAD/POPULARISED Jai Hind nationwide? " → Netaji Subash Chandra Bose. PYQ VAO 2006 and 2016: "Who first gave the slogan Jai Hind? " → Chembakaramam Pillai (1933, Vienna). "Who spread it? Two questions, two answers — do NOT mix.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: 🏃 The Great Escape — 1941 While under house arrest in Calcutta (1940–41), Bose planned daring escape Shaved clean, then wore fake beard + moustache to look like a Muslim man Dressed in large Afghan Muslim robe (colorful full-length garment) and turban False identity: Rahmat Khan Escaped from Calcutta home → through Afghanistan (contact: Bhagat Ram Talwar) → Germany In Germany, met Hitler who offered limited support and suggested Japan 💎 GEM Bose's disguise identity: Rahmat Khan — Afghan Muslim traveller with fake beard, robe, turban.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: Contact in Afghanistan: Bhagat Ram Talwar . 💎 GEM Lakshmi Sahgal — from Tamil Nadu, went to Singapore to study medicine in 1943, joined INA Women's Regiment, became its commander. In 2002, Congress nominated her as their Presidential candidate against Abdul Kalam (who won). Two massive historical connections in one person!

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: 🎯 Goals Achieve complete independence for India — NO compromise (opposed Dominion Status) Use military force if necessary — "Give me blood, I'll give you freedom" Unite all Indians regardless of religion and caste under the INA (Jai Hind slogan was deliberately non-Hindu) Liberate India through a military march from the northeast (Chalo Delhi! ) Establish a free, independent India's provisional government from abroad Champion armed revolutionary approach alongside Congress's peaceful methods 📰 Newspapers & Publications Bose did not found any

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: " broadcasts — reached millions of Indians The Azad Hind Government had its own official communications TIP Unlike Periyar (8 newspapers) or Nehru (The Independent, National Herald), Bose did NOT found newspapers. His primary communication was through radio broadcasts and books.

chunks.title: Subash Chandra Bose (Netaji)
chunks.text: 📚 Books & Writings Title Year Key Theme The Indian Struggle 1942 Book about India's freedom movement; written during Germany period Indian Sanyasi (Indian Ascetic) — Second major book; his philosophical reflections Thoughts of a Nation (Dēcattin Yōcanaigaḷ / தேசத்தின் யோசனைகள்) — His ideas and thoughts on nationhood and India's future Letters to Emilie Schenkl — Letters to his Austrian wife; compiled as book — similar to how Nehru's jail letters became Glimpses TIP Four books: The Indian Struggle, Indian Sanyasi, Thoughts of a Nation, Letters
